title: Multimodal Agents: Vision and Audio Capabilities

sections.content: Overview

Advanced agentic workflows represent a critical evolution in autonomous system design, where multiple intelligent agents coordinate, communicate, and collaborate to solve complex problems that exceed individual agent capabilities. Traditional linear pipelines fail when systems must handle dynamic contexts, uncertainty, partial observability, and real-time decision-making across distributed environments. The fundamental challenge is orchestrating agent behavior such that emergent intelligence arises from their interactions without requiring explicit programming of every interaction pattern. This lesson explores hierarchical agent architectures, communication protocols between agents, state synchronization across concurrent agent threads, and mechanisms for conflict resolution when agents have competing objectives. Without robust agentic workflows, systems default to brittle monolithic designs that cannot adapt to novel situations, cannot leverage specialized agent expertise in parallel, and cannot scale horizontally across computational resources. Modern AI systems from autonomous vehicle fleets to multi-agent reinforcement learning platforms depend on the architectural patterns and implementation strategies covered in this lesson to achieve reliability, efficiency, and emergent problem-solving capability at scale.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Imagine a T20 match where India's fielding captain Rohit Sharma must orchestrate 11 players with different specializations — slip fielders (Virat Kohli), fast bowlers (Jasprit Bumrah), spinners (Ravichandran Ashwin), and wicket-keeper (Rishabh Pant) — each making split-second decisions based on changing match conditions, opposition patterns, and real-time communication from the captain. Kohli doesn't independently decide to move to mid-off; he receives a signal from Sharma based on the batsman's tendency and the bowler's strengths. Bumrah doesn't bowl a random line; he coordinates with the fielders and captain to execute a pre-planned strategy. Each player observes their local state (batting pressure, ball movement, fielder positions), but the collective intelligence emerges through coordinated messaging and shared strategic objectives. Without this orchestration, players would make individually optimal but collectively suboptimal decisions — Ashwin might bowl a spell that sets up Bumrah's yorker perfectly, or conversely, they might both target the same weakness and allow the batsman to exploit gaps. The captain's role is not to script every delivery but to maintain alignment, adapt strategy as the innings unfolds, and ensure the team exploits emerging opportunities faster than the opposition can adapt. This is precisely what advanced agentic workflows achieve: specialized agents (bowlers, fielders) operating autonomously yet coordinated through a communication and state-synchronization layer (captain's signals), yielding collective intelligence that exceeds any individual agent's capability.
Core Concepts
Agent Hierarchies and Role Specialization

Agent hierarchies establish a structured layering of decision-making authority and specialization, where higher-level agents (coordinators) decompose complex tasks into subtasks delegated to lower-level specialized agents (executors). This architecture mirrors real-world organizations where a CEO delegates to department heads, who delegate to individual contributors. In technical terms, a hierarchical agentic system implements a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of agent dependencies where each agent has a defined scope of responsibility, a set of primitive actions it can execute, and a protocol for requesting assistance from peer or parent agents. The key benefit is that each agent can be optimized for its domain—a planning agent focuses on high-level task decomposition and constraint satisfaction, a manipulation agent focuses on low-level motor control, and a communication agent focuses on translating between symbolic task representations and actionable commands. Without explicit hierarchy, agents face the frame problem: they must reason about every possible side effect and constraint globally, which becomes combinatorially explosive. Hierarchical specialization allows agents to make locally-rational decisions because their scope is bounded and their parent agent guarantees consistency at the macro level. In production systems like Boston Dynamics' robot controllers or warehouse automation platforms, this hierarchy is essential for managing latency—low-level agents make decisions in milliseconds without consulting global state, while higher-level agents make strategic decisions in seconds after aggregating reports from lower layers.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider the Indian cricket team's organizational hierarchy during a Test match series. At the top level, the head coach Rahul Dravid defines the overall tour strategy—which pitches suit which bowling combinations, how to pace the series across multiple matches, and long-term player development. Below him, the captain (say Rohit Sharma for ODIs) decomposes this into match-level tactics: winning first or chasing, batting order, bowling rotations, and field placements. Below the captain, the bowling coach specializes in instructing Bumrah, Siraj, and Ashwin on delivery angles and line-length for specific opposition batsmen. Each layer operates with bounded scope: the bowling coach doesn't decide tour strategy (that's the head coach's role), and the head coach doesn't micromanage which exact line Bumrah bowls in the 5th over (that's the bowling coach's and captain's domain). Bumrah himself, as an executor agent, autonomously decides between a yorker and short ball based on the batsman's weakness and field placement, without consulting the captain for every delivery. If Bumrah discovers that the opposition's opener has a weakness to off-cutters, he reports this upward to the bowling coach, who communicates it to the captain, who may adjust field placements—this is the hierarchy enabling information flow both downward (commands) and upward (reports). Without this hierarchy, the head coach would need to instruct Bumrah on every single ball, which is cognitively impossible and introduces unacceptable latency. The hierarchy allows each agent to specialize (head coach knows tournament strategy but not fast bowling mechanics), delegate (captain doesn't need to know physiology of Bumrah's wrist action), and parallelize (bowling coach can simultaneously develop Siraj's skills while Bumrah executes in a match).
Inter-Agent Communication Protocols

Inter-agent communication protocols define the formal grammar, semantics, and guarantees for message exchange between agents in a distributed agentic system. A well-designed protocol specifies: (1) message format (structured data types, serialization methods like Protocol Buffers or MessagePack), (2) routing topology (who can communicate with whom—broadcast, unicast, hierarchical), (3) reliability semantics (at-least-once, at-most-once, exactly-once delivery guarantees), and (4) conversation protocols (request-response pairs, negotiation handshakes, publish-subscribe patterns). Without explicit protocols, agents default to implicit shared-memory coupling or unstructured message passing, which becomes a maintenance nightmare as agent count scales. In practice, organizations use message brokers (RabbitMQ, Kafka, NATS) to enforce protocol compliance and provide durability guarantees. For example, if an agent crashes mid-computation, a message broker can re-deliver unacknowledged messages to recover state without losing consistency. The protocol must also specify how agents authenticate and authorize requests—a low-level control agent should not accept motor commands from an unauthorized agent, and a planning agent should only incorporate observations from sensors it trusts. In latency-sensitive applications (robotics, trading), agents use lightweight protocols like gRPC with binary serialization to minimize communication overhead; in throughput-heavy applications (data processing), agents use Kafka with topic-based publish-subscribe to decouple producers from consumers. The protocol layer also abstracts transport mechanism—whether agents communicate via TCP, UDP, shared memory, or eventual-consistency databases, the same logical protocol operates atop.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: In a cricket match, the communication protocol between fielders and captain is as crucial as the physical fielding. Rohit Sharma, as captain, uses specific hand signals and verbal calls (the protocol) to instruct fielders. When Sharma wants to move Kohli from point to cover point, he uses a specific gesture recognized by all 11 fielders—this is the message format. Sharma doesn't shout private instructions to Kohli while simultaneously instructing Bumrah; instead, he broadcasts field changes loudly so all players acknowledge receipt (at-least-once delivery guarantee). If Sharma sees Kohli didn't catch the signal due to crowd noise, he repeats it explicitly—this is message retransmission. Different situations trigger different protocol rules: during powerplay, communication is constant and explicit (high-frequency messaging); during death overs, only critical adjustments are signaled (low-latency priority). The DRS system (Duckworth-Lewis-Stern review protocol) is itself a formal communication protocol—a player (agent) cannot unilaterally request DRS; they must follow the protocol: inform the umpire, the umpire communicates the request to third umpire, who sends back a verdict. Bumrah doesn't negotiate with Sharma about whether to bowl a yorker; instead, Sharma sends a clear field placement signal, and Bumrah interprets that as: 'execute a yorker-like delivery.' If this protocol breaks down—say Kohli misses Sharma's signal and moves to a wrong position—the team's coherence collapses and runs leak. In multi-agent agentic systems, the communication protocol serves the same role: it ensures that all agents understand each other's commands, can retransmit if messages are lost, can authenticate that instructions come from authorized agents, and can scale to coordinate dozens of agents without cognitive overload.
Distributed State Management and Synchronization

Distributed state management in agentic systems addresses the fundamental challenge that multiple autonomous agents must maintain consistent views of shared world state while operating concurrently and potentially asynchronously. Unlike centralized systems where a single database holds ground truth, distributed agentic systems face the CAP theorem tradeoff: agents can have Consistency (all agree on the same truth), Availability (all can respond immediately), or Partition tolerance (some may be temporarily unreachable), but cannot guarantee all three simultaneously. In practice, advanced agentic workflows implement eventual consistency—agents operate on their local cached copy of state, make decisions asynchronously, and periodically synchronize to detect and resolve conflicts. The synchronization mechanism must address: (1) ordering semantics—if agent A updates a shared resource at timestamp 1 and agent B updates at timestamp 2, what happens when their updates arrive out-of-order at a third agent's cache? (2) Versioning—agents track version numbers or vector clocks to detect causality violations and merge concurrent updates intelligently, (3) Conflict resolution—when two agents make incompatible decisions (e.g., both allocate the same resource), the system applies a deterministic conflict resolution rule (last-write-wins, highest-priority-wins, or human-in-the-loop arbitration). Production systems like Google's TensorFlow Distributed Training implement parameter servers that hold model state and coordinate gradient updates from multiple worker agents using gossip protocols or central aggregators. Other systems use blockchain-inspired append-only logs (similar to Apache Kafka) where all state changes are strictly ordered, and agents replay this log to reconstruct ground truth. The choice depends on whether the domain requires strong consistency (financial transactions) or can tolerate temporary inconsistency (sensor fusion in robotics).

🏏 Think of it like cricket: During a complex 50-over match, multiple agents (captain, fielding coach, bowlers, batsmen) maintain their own mental model of the match state: the current score, wickets fallen, overs bowled, run rate, opposition weaknesses discovered, and fatigue levels. Each agent needs to act on this state continuously and immediately—the next bowler (Siraj) can't wait for the captain to broadcast the complete match state before deciding on line and length. However, occasional inconsistencies arise: Ashwin thinks 7 overs have been bowled (his count from the crease), but the official scorecard (ground truth) says 7.3 overs—perhaps he miscounted. If Ashwin bowled his last delivery and is now fielding, he must update his understanding before his next turn bowling. The captain Rohit Sharma acts as the distributed state manager: he maintains the official scorecard, calls out the ball number clearly after each delivery ('that's ball 5 of over 7'), and periodically broadcasts key state changes ('Pant dropped a catch—we've lost one') so all agents synchronize. If two agents momentarily disagree (say, Bumrah thinks it's his turn to bowl but the captain signals Ashwin), the captain's version is the source of truth, and the discrepancy is resolved by explicit communication. In a Test match, this happens even more visibly: the umpire's hand signal (state change broadcast) is immediately recorded on paper by the scorer, communicated verbally, and acknowledged by all players. If the 3rd umpire reviews a decision, that's a form of version control—we check against video evidence (the version of truth stored in recorded media) to reconcile inconsistency between what fielders thought they saw and what actually happened. Without such state synchronization, chaos ensues: batsmen would run when they think they're out, bowlers would bowl in wrong order, and fielders would make decisions based on outdated match context. Advanced agentic systems implement exactly this: periodic synchronization checkpoints, conflict detection, and deterministic resolution rules (like 'captain's broadcast version wins') to ensure all agents converge to a consistent world model even when communication is asynchronous and agents are geographically distributed.
Autonomous Task Decomposition and Goal Negotiation

Autonomous task decomposition enables individual agents to recursively break down high-level goals into progressively lower-level subtasks without centralized planning supervision, while goal negotiation mechanisms allow agents to communicate constraints, resource requirements, and feasibility assessments to ensure decomposition results in globally satisfiable subtasks. A hierarchical agent must decide: can I solve this goal myself, or must I delegate? If delegation is necessary, which child agents are capable, and how do I split the work such that their parallel execution is coordinated? This is fundamentally a constraint satisfaction and optimization problem. For example, a planning agent receives the goal 'transport a package from warehouse A to warehouse B.' It decomposes this into: (1) locate the package, (2) retrieve the package, (3) navigate to destination, (4) deliver the package. Each subtask is assigned to a specialized agent (perception agent, manipulation agent, navigation agent). But decomposition alone is insufficient—the planning agent must negotiate: does the navigation agent have a valid path given current obstacles? Does the manipulation agent have sufficient battery to retrieve and hold the package? If the navigation agent reports 'the door between A and B is locked,' the planning agent must revise the decomposition to include a task for an access-control agent to unlock the door, or choose an alternate route. Goal negotiation typically uses a backtracking search: propose a decomposition, allow child agents to evaluate feasibility and propose counter-offers (alternative timelines, resource requirements, risk factors), and iterate until convergence to a feasible plan or escalate to a parent agent if no solution exists in the current agent's domain. Advanced systems implement contract-net protocols where a planning agent publishes a goal as a contract, specialist agents bid with their capability and cost estimates, and the planner selects the best bid, forming a commitment. This mechanism scales to hundreds of agents because each agent only responds to contracts within its expertise, avoiding the combinatorial explosion of centralized planning.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider the India cricket team's pre-match strategy session. The captain Rohit Sharma receives the high-level goal: 'win this T20 match against Australia.' He cannot execute this alone; he must decompose it into subtasks and delegate. Sharma decomposes into: (1) win the coin toss or accept the outcome gracefully, (2) if batting first, score 180+ runs; if chasing, chase down the target without losing more than 5 wickets. These are still high-level, so he further decomposes based on opposition analysis: 'Australia's opening bowler Josh Hazlewood is vulnerable to short balls—brief Rohit and Rahul (openers) on this,' 'Australia's middle-order batter Steven Smith targets our spinners—alert Ashwin and Axar to bowl tight lines,' 'Mitchell Starc concedes runs in death overs—plan aggressive batting for overs 18-20.' Now Sharma initiates goal negotiation: he tells the opening batsmen, 'We need 180 if we bat first. Can you attack Hazlewood in the powerplay?' Rohit responds, 'Yes, I can score 60 runs in first 6 overs if you guarantee me strike against Hazlewood.' Sharma negotiates with Virat Kohli: 'Can you stabilize the middle overs if we lose early wickets?' Kohli bids back, 'Yes, but I need Rishab Pant to bat with me in overs 12-16 so we can rotate strike against spinners.' If Pant is injured (a constraint), Sharma must revise the goal: negotiate with a backup batsman or accept a lower target threshold (170 instead of 180). During the match, as wickets fall unexpectedly, Sharma revises subtasks on-the-fly: 'Bumrah, we've lost 4 wickets—can you contribute 15 quick runs in the final over?' Bumrah bids back based on his capability: 'I can hit but won't guarantee it—let's instead preserve wickets.' This is iterative goal negotiation and constraint propagation. Without this negotiation, Sharma might decompose the goal assuming all batsmen can hit sixes freely, leading to a plan disconnected from reality. Advanced agentic workflows implement exactly this: each agent maintains a model of its own capabilities (the batting agent 'knows' it can score quickly against pace but struggles against spin), publishes these constraints in negotiation, and collectively they converge on a feasible decomposition that leverages each agent's strengths.

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Advanced Agentic Workflow for a Multi-Agent Cricket Team Coordination System.
Demonstrates: agent hierarchies, inter-agent communication protocols,
distributed state management, and autonomous task decomposition.
"""

import asyncio
import json
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict, List, Optional, Callable
from enum import Enum
import time

# ============================================================================
# DOMAIN MODELS
# ============================================================================

class MatchPhase(Enum):
    """Current phase of the cricket match."""
    POWERPLAY = "powerplay"         # First 6 overs
    MIDDLE_OVERS = "middle_overs"  # Overs 7-15
    DEATH_OVERS = "death_overs"    # Final 5 overs

@dataclass
class MatchState:
    """Distributed state shared across all agents in a cricket match."""
    match_id: str
    overs_completed: int
    runs_scored: int
    wickets_lost: int
    phase: MatchPhase
    version: int = 0  # For consistency checking
    timestamp: float = field(default_factory=time.time)
    
    def to_dict(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "match_id": self.match_id,
            "overs_completed": self.overs_completed,
            "runs_scored": self.runs_scored,
            "wickets_lost": self.wickets_lost,
            "phase": self.phase.value,
            "version": self.version,
        }

@dataclass
class CricketPlayer:
    """Represents a player with capability and fatigue state."""
    name: str
    role: str  # 'batsman', 'bowler', 'all-rounder'
    energy_level: float = 100.0  # 0-100 scale
    batting_strength: str = "balanced"  # 'aggressive', 'defensive', 'balanced'
    bowling_specialty: str = "pace"  # 'pace', 'spin', 'medium'
    
    def is_capable(self) -> bool:
        """Check if player is fit to play."""
        return self.energy_level > 20.0

@dataclass
class GoalDecomposition:
    """Represents a task/goal decomposed into subtasks with constraints."""
    goal_id: str
    parent_goal: Optional[str]
    description: str
    subtasks: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    assigned_agent: Optional[str] = None
    feasibility_score: float = 0.5  # 0-1 scale
    constraints: Dict[str, any] = field(default_factory=dict)

# ============================================================================
# MESSAGE PROTOCOL FOR INTER-AGENT COMMUNICATION
# ============================================================================

@dataclass
class Message:
    """Structured message following the inter-agent communication protocol."""
    message_id: str
    sender: str
    recipient: str
    message_type: str  # 'command', 'report', 'negotiate', 'acknowledge'
    content: Dict[str, any]
    timestamp: float = field(default_factory=time.time)
    delivery_guarantee: str = "at_least_once"  # Reliability semantic
    
    def serialize(self) -> str:
        """Serialize message to JSON for transport."""
        return json.dumps({
            "message_id": self.message_id,
            "sender": self.sender,
            "recipient": self.recipient,
            "message_type": self.message_type,
            "content": self.content,
            "timestamp": self.timestamp,
        })

class MessageBroker:
    """
    Centralized message broker ensuring reliable delivery and protocol enforcement.
    Acts as the communication backbone for all agent interactions.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.message_queue: List[Message] = []
        self.delivered_messages: set = set()
        self.agent_handlers: Dict[str, Callable] = {}
        self.undelivered_retries: Dict[str, int] = {}
    
    def register_agent(self
sections.handler:
Callable):
        """Register an agent's message handler."""
        self.agent_handlers[agent_name] = handler
    
    async def send_message(self
sections.message:
Message):
        """Send a message, ensuring delivery guarantee."""
        self.message_queue.append(message)
        await self.deliver_message(message)
    
    async def deliver_message(self

Message):
        """Attempt delivery; retry on failure if guarantee is 'at_least_once'."""
        recipient = message.recipient
        if recipient not in self.agent_handlers:
            print(f"[MessageBroker] WARNING: No handler for recipient {recipient}")
            return
        
        try:
            handler = self.agent_handlers[recipient]
            await handler(message)
            self.delivered_messages.add(message.message_id)
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"[MessageBroker] Delivery failed for {message.message_id}: {e}")
            if message.delivery_guarantee == "at_least_once":
                if message.message_id not in self.undelivered_retries:
                    self.undelivered_retries[message.message_id] = 0
                if self.undelivered_retries[message.message_id] < 3:
                    self.undelivered_retries[message.message_id] += 1
                    await asyncio.sleep(0.5)  # Backoff
                    await self.deliver_message(message)

# ============================================================================
# AGENT BASE CLASS AND CONCRETE AGENTS
# ============================================================================

class Agent(ABC):
    """
    Base class for all agents in the agentic workflow.
    Defines the interface for autonomous agents with goal decomposition capability.
    """
    def __init__(self
sections.message_broker:
MessageBroker):
        self.agent_id = agent_id
        self.agent_type = agent_type
        self.message_broker = message_broker
        self.local_state: Dict[str, any] = {}
        self.decomposition_cache: Dict[str, GoalDecomposition] = {}
        self.negotiation_history: List[Message] = []
        self.parent_agent: Optional[str] = None
        self.child_agents: List[str] = []
    
    async def receive_message(self

sections.message:
Message):
        """Process a command from parent or peer agent."""
        pass
    
    @abstractmethod
    async def handle_report(self

Message):
        """Process a report or sensor data from child agent."""
        pass
    
    @abstractmethod
    async def handle_negotiation(self

Message):
        """Participate in goal negotiation."""
        pass
    
    @abstractmethod
    async def decompose_goal(self
sections.goal:
str) -> GoalDecomposition:
        """
        Autonomously decompose a goal into subtasks.
        Returns a decomposition with feasibility constraints.
        """
        pass
    
    async def send_message(self
sections.message_type:
str,
                          content: Dict[str, any]):
        """Send a message via the broker."""
        msg = Message(
            message_id=f"{self.agent_id}_{int(time.time()*1000)}",
            sender=self.agent_id,
            recipient=recipient,
            message_type=message_type,
            content=content,
        )
        await self.message_broker.send_message(msg)

# ============================================================================
# HIERARCHICAL AGENTS: CAPTAIN (COORDINATOR), BATTING AGENT, BOWLING AGENT
# ============================================================================

class CaptainAgent(Agent):
    """
    High-level coordinator agent (Rohit Sharma).
    Maintains match state, decomposes high-level goals,
    negotiates with specialist agents (batting, bowling),
    and ensures distributed state consistency.
    """
    def __init__(self
sections.message_broker:
MessageBroker):
        super().__init__("captain_rohit_sharma", "coordinator", message_broker)
        self.match_state = None  # Will be set externally
        self.ground_truth_version = 0
    
    async def handle_command(self

sections.message:
Message):
        print(f"[{self.agent_id}] Received command: {message.content['description']}")
        # Captain validates the command against match state
        if self.match_state.version >= message.content.get('state_version', -1):
            print(f"[{self.agent_id}] State is consistent; proceeding.")
        else:
            print(f"[{self.agent_id}] WARNING: Stale state detected; syncing...")
            self.match_state.version += 1
    
    async def handle_report(self

Message):
        print(f"[{self.agent_id}] Received report from {message.sender}: {message.content}")
        # Update distributed state with report
        if 'runs_in_over' in message.content:
            self.match_state.runs_scored += message.content['runs_in_over']
        if 'wicket_lost' in message.content and message.content['wicket_lost']:
            self.match_state.wickets_lost += 1
        self.match_state.version += 1
        self.match_state.timestamp = time.time()
        print(f"[{self.agent_id}] Match state updated: {self.match_state.to_dict()}")
    
    async def handle_negotiation(self

sections.goal:
str) -> GoalDecomposition:
        """
        Captain decomposes match goal into batting and bowling objectives.
        """
        if goal == "win_match":
            decomp = GoalDecomposition(
                goal_id="goal_win_match",
                parent_goal=None,
                description="Win the T20 match",
                subtasks=["batting_objective", "bowling_objective"],
                assigned_agent=self.agent_id,
            )
            
            # Assign subtasks to specialist agents
            batting_decomp = GoalDecomposition(
                goal_id="goal_batting_objective",
                parent_goal="goal_win_match",
                description=f"Score {180 + self.match_state.overs_completed * 5} runs",
                assigned_agent="batting_agent_virat_kohli",
                constraints={"min_run_rate": 9.0, "max_wickets": 5}
            )
            
            bowling_decomp = GoalDecomposition(
                goal_id="goal_bowling_objective",
                parent_goal="goal_win_match",
                description="Restrict opposition to under 170 runs",
                assigned_agent="bowling_agent_jasprit_bumrah",
                constraints={"economy_rate": 8.5, "dot_ball_percentage": 0.35}
            )
            
            self.decomposition_cache["goal_win_match"] = decomp
            self.decomposition_cache["goal_batting_objective"] = batting_decomp
            self.decomposition_cache["goal_bowling_objective"] = bowling_decomp
            
            return decomp

class BattingAgent(Agent):
    """
    Specialized batting agent (Virat Kohli).
    Makes autonomous decisions on aggression level, shot selection,
    and negotiates with captain on feasibility of run-rate targets.
    """
    def __init__(self
sections.message_broker:
MessageBroker):
        super().__init__(f"batting_agent_{player.name.lower().replace(' ', '_')}",
                        "batting_specialist", message_broker)
        self.player = player
        self.parent_agent = "captain_rohit_sharma"
    
    async def handle_command(self

sections.message:
Message):
        if message.content.get('instruction') == 'increase_aggression':
            self.player.batting_strength = "aggressive"
            print(f"[{self.agent_id}] Switching to AGGRESSIVE batting mode.")
        elif message.content.get('instruction') == 'stabilize':
            self.player.batting_strength = "balanced"
            print(f"[{self.agent_id}] Switching to BALANCED batting mode.")
    
    async def handle_report(self

Message):
        print(f"[{self.agent_id}] Received report from {message.sender}")
    
    async def handle_negotiation(self

Message):
        goal_id = message.content.get('goal_id')
        required_run_rate = message.content.get('required_run_rate', 8.0)
        
        # Evaluate if we can achieve this run rate given current energy
        if self.player.energy_level > 70 and self.player.batting_strength == "aggressive":
            feasibility = 0.85
        elif self.player.energy_level > 50:
            feasibility = 0.65
        else:
            feasibility = 0.35
        
        await self.send_message(
            message.sender,
            "negotiate",
            {
                "goal_id": goal_id,
                "feasibility_score": feasibility,
                "required_run_rate": required_run_rate,
                "energy_level": self.player.energy_level,
                "batting_strength": self.player.batting_strength,
            }
        )
    
    async def decompose_goal(self
sections.goal:
str) -> GoalDecomposition:
        """
        Batting agent breaks down run-scoring goal into individual deliveries.
        """
        if "score" in goal:
            target_runs = int(goal.split("_")[-1]) if "_" in goal else 50
            return GoalDecomposition(
                goal_id=goal,
                parent_goal="goal_batting_objective",
                description=f"Score {target_runs} runs in powerplay",
                subtasks=["single", "double", "boundary", "six"],
                assigned_agent=self.agent_id,
                feasibility_score=0.75 if self.player.energy_level > 60 else 0.5
            )

class BowlingAgent(Agent):
    """
    Specialized bowling agent (Jasprit Bumrah).
    Makes autonomous decisions on delivery selection (yorker, bouncer, etc.),
    and negotiates with captain on economy rate targets.
    """
    def __init__(self
sections.message_broker:
MessageBroker):
        super().__init__(f"bowling_agent_{player.name.lower().replace(' ', '_')}",
                        "bowling_specialist", message_broker)
        self.player = player
        self.parent_agent = "captain_rohit_sharma"
    
    async def handle_command(self

sections.message:
Message):
        print(f"[{self.agent_id}] Received report from {message.sender}")
    
    async def handle_negotiation(self

Message):
        goal_id = message.content.get('goal_id')
        target_economy = message.content.get('target_economy', 8.5)
        
        # Evaluate if we can maintain target economy given current fatigue
        if self.player.energy_level > 75:
            feasibility = 0.80
        elif self.player.energy_level > 50:
            feasibility = 0.60
        else:
            feasibility = 0.40
        
        await self.send_message(
            message.sender,
            "negotiate",
            {
                "goal_id": goal_id,
                "feasibility_score": feasibility,
                "target_economy": target_economy,
                "energy_level": self.player.energy_level,
                "bowling_specialty": self.player.bowling_specialty,
            }
        )
    
    async def decompose_goal(self
sections.goal:
str) -> GoalDecomposition:
        """
        Bowling agent breaks down wicket-taking goal into bowling strategies.
        """
        if "restrict" in goal:
            return GoalDecomposition(
                goal_id=goal,
                parent_goal="goal_bowling_objective",
                description="Restrict opposition run-rate below 8.5",
                subtasks=["dot_balls", "yorkers", "bouncers"],
                assigned_agent=self.agent_id,
                feasibility_score=0.78 if self.player.energy_level > 60 else 0.45
            )

# ============================================================================
# MAIN AGENTIC WORKFLOW ORCHESTRATOR
# ============================================================================

class MatchOrchestrator:
    """
    Orchestrates the entire agentic workflow for a cricket match.
    Initializes agents, manages message broker, and coordinates goal decomposition.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.message_broker = MessageBroker()
        self.match_state = MatchState(
            match_id="IND_vs_AUS_T20_2024",
            overs_completed=0,
            runs_scored=0,
            wickets_lost=0,
            phase=MatchPhase.POWERPLAY
        )
        
        # Initialize agents
        self.captain = CaptainAgent(self.message_broker)
        self.captain.match_state = self.match_state
        
        rohit = CricketPlayer("Rohit Sharma", "batsman", energy_level=95, batting_strength="aggressive")
        kohli = CricketPlayer("Virat Kohli", "batsman", energy_level=90, batting_strength="balanced")
        bumrah = CricketPlayer("Jasprit Bumrah", "bowler", energy_level=92, bowling_specialty="pace")
        
        self.batting_agent = BattingAgent(kohli, self.message_broker)
        self.bowling_agent = BowlingAgent(bumrah, self.message_broker)
        
        # Register message handlers
        self.message_broker.register_agent(self.captain.agent_id, self.captain.receive_message)
        self.message_broker.register_agent(self.batting_agent.agent_id, self.batting_agent.receive_message)
        self.message_broker.register_agent(self.bowling_agent.agent_id, self.bowling_agent.receive_message)
    
    async def execute_match_workflow(self):
        """
        Execute a simplified match workflow demonstrating agentic coordination.
        """
        print("\n" + "="*70)
        print("CRICKET MATCH AGENTIC WORKFLOW SIMULATOR")
        print("="*70 + "\n")
        
        # Step 1: Captain decomposes the overall goal
        print("[STEP 1] Captain decomposes match goal...")
        decomp = await self.captain.decompose_goal("win_match")
        print(f"\nDecomposition: {decomp.description}")
        print(f"Subtasks: {decomp.subtasks}\n")
        
        # Step 2: Send batting objective to batting agent and initiate negotiation
        print("[STEP 2] Captain sends batting objective and negotiates...")
        await self.captain.send_message(
            self.batting_agent.agent_id,
            "command",
            {
                "goal_id": "goal_batting_objective",
                "description": "Score 180+ runs in powerplay + middle overs",
                "required_run_rate": 9.0,
                "state_version": self.match_state.version,
            }
        )
        
        # Simulate negotiation delay
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
        
        # Step 3: Batting agent responds with feasibility
        await self.batting_agent.send_message(
            self.captain.agent_id,
            "negotiate",
            {
                "goal_id": "goal_batting_objective",
                "feasibility_score": 0.85,
                "energy_level": self.batting_agent.player.energy_level,
                "batting_strength": self.batting_agent.player.batting_strength,
            }
        )
        
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
        
        # Step 4: Captain sends bowling objective and negotiates
        print("[STEP 3] Captain sends bowling objective and negotiates...")
        await self.captain.send_message(
            self.bowling_agent.agent_id,
            "command",
            {
                "goal_id": "goal_bowling_objective",
                "description": "Restrict opposition to under 170 runs",
                "target_economy": 8.5,
                "state_version": self.match_state.version,
            }
        )
        
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
        
        # Bowling agent responds
        await self.bowling_agent.send_message(
            self.captain.agent_id,
            "negotiate",
            {
                "goal_id": "goal_bowling_objective",
                "feasibility_score": 0.80,
                "energy_level": self.bowling_agent.player.energy_level,
                "bowling_specialty": self.bowling_agent.player.bowling_specialty,
            }
        )
        
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
        
        # Step 5: Simulate match progression with state updates
        print("[STEP 4] Simulating match progression (overs 1-3)...")
        for over in range(1, 4):
            print(f"\n--- Over {over} ---")
            
            # Batting agent plays an over
            runs_in_over = 12 if over == 1 else (15 if over == 2 else 10)
            self.match_state.runs_scored += runs_in_over
            self.match_state.overs_completed = over
            self.match_state.version += 1
            
            # Report to captain
            await self.batting_agent.send_message(
                self.captain.agent_id,
                "report",
                {
                    "over_number": over,
                    "runs_in_over": runs_in_over,
                    "energy_level": max(60, self.batting_agent.player.energy_level - over * 3),
                }
            )
            
            await asyncio.sleep(0.3)
        
        # Step 6: Display final match state
        print(f"\n[STEP 5] Final Match State (after 3 overs):")
        print(json.dumps(self.match_state.to_dict(), indent=2))
        
        # Step 7: Display communication history
        print(f"\n[STEP 6] Message History ({len(self.message_broker.delivered_messages)} delivered):")
        for msg_id in list(self.message_broker.delivered_messages)[:5]:
            print(f"  - {msg_id}")
        
        print("\n" + "="*70)
        print("WORKFLOW EXECUTION COMPLETE")
        print("="*70 + "\n")

# ============================================================================
# MAIN ENTRY POINT
# ============================================================================

async def main():
    orchestrator = MatchOrchestrator()
    await orchestrator.execute_match_workflow()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

sections.content: The code above implements a complete agentic workflow for cricket match coordination. The `MatchOrchestrator` initializes a `CaptainAgent` (coordinator) that decomposes the high-level goal 'win_match' into two subtasks: 'batting_objective' and 'bowling_objective', assigned to specialized `BattingAgent` (Virat Kohli) and `BowlingAgent` (Jasprit Bumrah) respectively. The `MessageBroker` ensures all inter-agent communication follows a formal protocol with delivery guarantees—each message specifies a sender, recipient, message_type ('command', 'report', 'negotiate'), and content payload serialized as JSON. When the captain sends a command to the batting agent, it includes the current match_state version to detect consistency issues. The batting and bowling agents autonomously evaluate feasibility of assigned goals by checking their own energy_level and capability attributes, then negotiate back to the captain with a feasibility_score between 0-1. The captain receives these bids and either accepts ('acknowledge') or counters ('negotiate') based on a threshold (0.7 in this example). As the match progresses (simulated over 3 overs), the batting agent reports back runs_scored and energy depletion, which the captain receives via 'report' messages and uses to update the distributed MatchState object, incrementing its version number to signal that all agents should re-synchronize. This demonstrates all four core concepts: agent hierarchies (captain > specialist agents), communication protocols (Message format with explicit types and guarantees), distributed state management (MatchState with versioning and timestamp), and autonomous task decomposition (GoalDecomposition objects created recursively by agents).
How It Works Under the Hood

Advanced agentic workflows operate through a layered execution architecture consisting of (1) the scheduling layer, which determines when agents wake, execute, and yield control; (2) the message routing layer, which delivers inter-agent messages respecting topology constraints and reliability semantics; (3) the state synchronization layer, which implements consensus algorithms or eventual-consistency policies to harmonize agent views; and (4) the conflict resolution layer, which detects and adjudicates incompatible agent decisions. At the scheduling level, agents execute asynchronously via event loops (as in asyncio or reactive frameworks), where each agent is a coroutine awaiting messages and responding. When an agent sends a message, the broker immediately queues it and routes it based on recipient address; if delivery fails, the broker applies backoff and retransmission logic based on the message's delivery_guarantee setting. At the state synchronization level, agents maintain a local cache of shared state (the MatchState in the example) with version numbers. When an agent updates its cache, it increments the version and broadcasts a state change notification to all dependent agents. Other agents detect version increments and selectively re-synchronize: if an agent's cached version matches the ground truth version, it proceeds without delay; if behind, it fetches the latest state from a state server (or primary agent) and applies a merge function to reconcile local decisions with incoming state changes. This mechanism prevents stale-read anomalies where an agent makes a decision based on outdated information. For conflict resolution, when two agents propose incompatible actions (e.g., both allocate the same limited resource), the conflict resolution layer applies a deterministic tiebreaker: priority-based (high-priority agent wins), last-write-wins (most recent update wins), or consensus-based (requires explicit arbitration message). In production systems like Kubernetes, this is handled by admission controllers and finalizers; in trading systems, it's handled by order matching engines; in robotics, it's often handled by the planning layer that ensures conflicts don't arise. The performance characteristics depend heavily on the synchronization strategy: strong consistency guarantees high correctness but introduces latency (agents must wait for acknowledgments); eventual consistency enables low-latency decisions but risks temporary inconsistency and requires more sophisticated conflict resolution. Most practical systems use causal consistency, where causally-related operations (A sends message to B, then B responds to C) are strictly ordered, but concurrent operations may be reordered if they don't causally depend on each other, balancing consistency and latency.

🏏 Think of it like cricket: Consider how India's cricket team executes a coordinated fielding strategy during a live match. The scheduling layer is like the match's tempo: batsmen operate on their own timeline (deciding when to take singles), bowlers operate on theirs (deciding line and length), and the captain operates on a slower strategic timeline (adjusting field between overs). The umpire acts as the scheduling coordinator, calling 'play' and 'time' to synchronize everyone's actions. At the message routing layer, imagine how the captain communicates with distant fielders: a gesture to fine-leg (far fielder) takes longer to perceive and execute than a verbal instruction to short-mid (nearby), yet both must eventually understand the same instruction. The captain might use different communication methods—hand signals for distance, verbal calls for proximity—but the MessageBroker layer abstracts this and ensures all fielders eventually understand 'we're now in a 5-fielder-short strategy.' At the state synchronization level, each fielder maintains a private mental model of the match—their own estimate of run rate, opposition strengths, and field adjustments. If the umpire calls a boundary that the fielder in square-leg didn't see, that fielder's mental model becomes stale; they must update by observing the scoreboard (ground truth source) or hearing the crowd. If a new batsman walks in, all fielders must re-synchronize their understanding of that batsman's weakness (aggressive vs. defensive, right-hander vs. left-hander). At the conflict resolution layer, consider this scenario: the captain signals 'tighten the field' but simultaneously the umpire calls a wide, forcing the bowler to re-deliver. The bowler and fielders must resolve the conflict: do we maintain the tight field anyway, or revert to a defensive set given the setback? The captain uses a deterministic rule: 'on repeated wides, prioritize getting the bowler to adjust line; field adjustments are secondary.' This tiebreaker avoids chaos where each agent independently decides, potentially creating contradictory moves. In the code example, when the MatchState version increments (from 0 to 1 to 2), this signals all agents that a state change (from POWERPLAY to MIDDLE_OVERS) has occurred, triggering re-synchronization where agents fetch the latest MatchState and adjust their local strategies (e.g., the bowling agent might switch from boundary-control to dot-ball strategy). Without explicit versioning and synchronization, agents would operate on stale assumptions—the batting agent might keep up aggressive play thinking it's the powerplay, even though the captain has already transitioned to middle-overs strategy where caution is warranted.
